nativeRange.habitat: Leverett's Hillstream Loach inhabits fast-flowing, highly oxygenated hill streams and mountain rivers with gravel, cobble, and boulder substrates. Rock surfaces in these habitats accumulate a thin layer of biofilm and diatom growth — collectively called aufwuchs — which forms the basis of the species' diet. The Red and Pearl River drainages of southern China and Vietnam, including Hainan Island, represent the core of its native range.

decorPreferences: smooth flat rocks and river boulders for resting and aufwuchs grazing
cobble and pebble substrate allowing biofilm colonisation
powerful circulation pump or powerhead to simulate torrent current (10–15× turnover per hour)
mature tank with well-established diatom and biofilm growth on rock surfaces
low-profile decorations to preserve unobstructed flow lanes
fine sand or gravel interspersed between rocks for occasional substrate resting

diet.notes: Leverett's Hillstream Loach is a specialist aufwuchs grazer, scraping biofilm, diatoms, and micro-algae from rock surfaces in the wild. In captivity, natural biofilm growth on rocks and glass is an essential part of the diet, supplemented with spirulina wafers, algae tabs pressed against rocks or the aquarium glass, blanched zucchini or cucumber, and high-quality sinking algae-based pellets. Small frozen or live foods such as daphnia and baby brine shrimp are accepted as an occasional supplement. Plant-based foods should make up the bulk of the diet. This species can starve gradually in low-flow or recently set-up tanks where insufficient biofilm has developed, so strong circulation and allowing algae to colonise rock surfaces naturally are prerequisites to keeping it successfully.

goodWith: White Cloud Mountain Minnows (same cool-water preference)
other hillstream loaches (Sewellia, Gastromyzon — if same temperature range)
small cool-water danios (Zebra Danio, Celestial Pearl Danio)
dwarf shrimp (generally safe; monitor in small tanks)
avoidWith: tropical community fish requiring 26°C+ (Neon Tetras, Discus, Angelfish, Guppies — dangerously warm for this species)
large boisterous fish that outcompete for food at rock surfaces
fish requiring still or low-flow water
Beaufortia kweichowensis kept together without adequate space — intraspecific territorial posturing possible at feeding sites

imageCandidates.notes: Grégoire Germeau; 435x480. Classic dorsal view showing diagnostic olive-gold base with dark leopard spots, flattened body and fanned butterfly fins; low resolution is the only caveat.
Leo-nie-weh-oh; 3072x2304. High-res live in-tank shot but a ventral suction/belly view against glass, murky backlighting, dorsal spot pattern not visible; secondary only.

summary: Leverett’s Hillstream Loach is a flattened, torrent-adapted fish from fast-flowing rivers of southern China and Vietnam. It requires cool water between 18 and 24°C, powerful circulation, and a mature tank with abundant biofilm on rock surfaces — an intermediate-level species with strict flow, temperature, and dietary requirements.
careNotes: Leverett's Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia leveretti) is a torrent-adapted species from the Red and Pearl River drainages of southern China and Vietnam, including Hainan Island. It shares fundamental care requirements with its close relative Beaufortia kweichowensis, the Butterfly Hillstream Loach, but is distinguished by its distribution, slightly variable colouration (a golden base with dark spots), and a larger potential adult size of up to 10 cm, though most aquarium specimens are sold and kept at 5–7 cm. Care guidance developed for one species generally applies equally to the other. Temperature must be kept between 18 and 24°C; sustained exposure above 24–25°C depletes dissolved oxygen and causes gradual decline. Strong water movement is non-negotiable: target 10–15 times the tank volume per hour using a powerful powerhead or circulation pump, directed across flat rock surfaces where the fish spend most of their time. A minimum of 80 L with a 75 cm footprint is suitable for a small group, but wider tanks with greater rock surface area are strongly preferred. The most common cause of loss is starvation in tanks where aufwuchs (the thin layer of diatoms and biofilm that accumulates on illuminated rock surfaces) has not yet developed. This species does not consume macroalgae sheets as its primary food; it grazes the micro-layer that builds up in well-lit, high-flow, mature conditions. Supplement generously with spirulina wafers, algae tabs fixed to rocks or glass, and blanched vegetables. Water quality must be pristine: low nitrates, high dissolved oxygen, and regular partial water changes are essential. Keep in groups of at least three; minor territorial interactions at feeding surfaces are normal and rarely escalate beyond posturing. The species name honours Rev. William J. Leverett, an American Presbyterian missionary on Hainan Island from 1894 to 1924. It was formally described by Nichols and Pope in 1927 under the older name Gastromyzon leveretti, which remains a synonym.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning of Leverett's Hillstream Loach is rarely reported. Males are believed to develop minor nuptial tubercles — small raised bumps — on the snout when in breeding condition. The conditions most likely to trigger spawning include a mild seasonal temperature dip that simulates the winter-to-spring transition, increased current flow, and a highly varied diet rich in live aufwuchs and spirulina. A species-only hillstream biotope setup is generally considered the most practical context in which to attempt breeding. Eggs are deposited in crevices or under rocks in high-flow zones. Fry are tiny and require an abundant supply of micro-aufwuchs on nearby surfaces; cultured micro-algae or infusoria can supplement natural biofilm in the early stages. Breeding success remains uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: FishBase — Beaufortia leveretti
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), maximum size (12.0 cm TL, common 5.0 cm SL), native range (China and Vietnam: Red and Pearl River systems, Hainan Island), habitat (mountain streams with gravel bottom, demersal), temperature range (18–24°C), trophic level 2.6 (herbivore-leaning), and conservation status (Least Concern, IUCN 2010). Species description: Nichols & Pope, 1927; synonym Gastromyzon leveretti.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Beaufortia leveretti
additional.notes: Specialist hobbyist article dedicated to B. leveretti. Confirmed size (to 12 cm / 5 inches), temperature range (65–75°F / 18–24°C), water chemistry (moderately hard, neutral to slightly basic), dietary needs (plant-based bulk, sinking foods accepted), high-flow and oxygenation requirements, peaceful temperament, and limited captive breeding reports. Notes challenges with shipping stress on initial acquisition.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Hillstream Loach Week Part 2: Beaufortia leveretti
additional.notes: Trade importer’s species profile. Confirmed origin (China and Vietnam, imported from China), distinctive golden body with black spots, ideal seasonal temperature range (12°C winter, 21°C summer), suitability for unheated aquaria, and pairing with White Cloud Mountain Minnows (15–20 specimens in water column with 10–12 Beaufortia). Valuable source on real-world temperature seasonality.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Beaufortia leveretti
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), maximum length (12 cm), native range (Red and Pearl River systems, China and Vietnam, Hainan Island), and conservation status (Least Concern, IUCN 2010). Confirmed synonym Gastromyzon leveretti Nichols & Pope, 1927.

additional.name: Aqualapp — Beaufortia leveretti care and compatibility
additional.notes: Hobby-oriented profile specific to B. leveretti. Confirmed size range (6–10 cm), temperature (18–24°C), pH (6.6–7.5), softish-water preference (gH 5–15), peaceful temperament with possible minor conspecific territorial behaviour at feeding sites, group-keeping recommendation (minimum 3), and herbivorous diet. Minimum tank suggestion of 15 L in source is too low for practical group husbandry; 80 L used as practical minimum.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved. Conditioning typically involves a seasonal temperature dip to simulate the winter-to-spring transition, increased current flow, and a diet rich in live aufwuchs and spirulina. Eggs are deposited in rock crevices under high-flow conditions, and fry require an established supply of micro-aufwuchs on nearby surfaces. A species-only hillstream biotope setup is generally regarded as the most suitable approach for spawning attempts.
